pubs.acs.org/crt

![image 2](data:image/png;base64,)

Ac

Article

# A Weakly Supervised Deep Learning Framework for Whole Slide Classification to Facilitate Digital Pathology in Animal Study

Nicole Bussola, Joshua Xu, Leihong Wu, Lorenzo Gorini, Yifan Zhang, Cesare Furlanello, * and Weida Tong *

![image 4](data:image/png;base64,)

![image 3](data:image/png;base64,)

Cite This: Chem. Res. Toxicol. 2023, 36, 1321-1331

Read Online

![image 5](data:image/png;base64,)

ACCESS

*

Metrics & More

Article Recommendations

Supporting Information

sı

Injury prediction

WSI (weak label)

PathologAI

Lesion presence

Treatment-related

Injury severity

Dose-time

point

ABSTRACT: The pathology of animal studies is crucial for toxicity evaluations and regulatory assessments, but the manual examination of slides by pathologists remains time-consuming and requires extensive training. One inherent challenge in this process is the interobserver variability, which can compromise the consistency and accuracy of a study. Artificial intelligence (AI) has demonstrated its ability to automate similar examinations in clinical applications with enhanced efficiency, consistency, and accuracy. However, training AI models typically relies on costly pixel-level annotation of injured regions and is often not available for animal pathology. To address this, we developed the PathologAI system, a 'weakly' supervised approach for WSI classification in rat images without explicit lesion annotation at the pixel level. Using rat liver imaging data from the Open TG-GATEs system, PathologAI was applied to predict necrosis of n = 816 WSIs (377 controls). TG-GATEs studied 170 compounds at three dose levels (low, middle, and high) for four time points (3, 7, 14, and 28 days). PathologAI first preprocessed WSIs at the tile level to generate a high-level representation with a Generative Adversarial Network architecture. The prediction of liver necrosis relied on an ensemble model of 5 CNN classifiers trained on 335 WSIs. The ensemble model achieved notable classification accuracy on the holdout test set: 87% among 87 control slides free of findings, 83% among 120 controls with spontaneous necrosis, 67% among 147 treated animals with spontaneous minimal or slight necrosis, and 59% among 127 treated animals with minimal or slight necrosis caused by the treatment. Importantly, PathologAI was able to discriminate WSIs with spontaneous necrosis from those with treatment related necrosis and discriminated mild lesion level findings (slight vs minimal) and between treatment dose levels. PathologAI could provide an inexpensive and rapid screening tool to assist the digital pathology analysis in preclinical applications and general toxicological studies.

# ■ INTRODUCTION

Animal models are routinely used to conduct crucial safety evaluations of therapeutics and risk assessments of chemical products. Although significant efforts toward the development of alternative approaches to animal testing have been promoted globally, animal studies are still indispensable to understanding the toxicological effects of chemicals on physiological functions and systemic interactions between organs. 1 Pathological examination of organ tissues is a key step in animal studies to determine whether a chemical could cause lesions and their extent and severity within specific organs. This manual inspection, however, is a laborious process and requires extensive training. Additionally, different pathologists may adopt different guidelines. The subjective nature of manual pathological assessments is known to lead to significant discrepancies in results and remains an issue in digital imaging, which is supported by modern high-resolution scanners and

![image 6](data:image/png;base64,)

histopathology workflows. In diagnostic settings, it has been shown that the arbitrary selection of tissue regions to analyze in detail and the subjective evaluation of these findings can lead to low interpathologist agreement, 2,3 with 19% of cases generating results with less than or equal to 70% concordance for ERBB2 immuno-histochemistry scores, 4 thereby risking the misassignment of patients for treatment. For animal studies, the National Toxicology Program (NTP) has been created to mitigate the inherent subjectivity of histopathology assess-

Received:

Published:

February 24, 2023

August 4, 2023

![image 7](data:image/png;base64,)

![image 8](data:image/png;base64,)

W

A)

H

Packing and

padding

W

128

Figure 1. Visual summary of the PathologAI architecture experimental workflow. (A) WSIs are preprocessed into tiles; each tile is compressed with the Bidirectional Generative Adversarial Network (BiGAN) encoder into a vector of length L = 128. (B) The embedding maps the WSI into a compressed WSI, preserving the original local structure of the tissue. The transformed images are split into 5 data sets stratified by label, balanced for topography type, and segregated by original animal experiment. Five CNN classifiers are trained with the WSI label as target value to provide an injury score, defined as the mean probability of positive class. An ensemble model is used for the final prediction.

ments. 5 Further, the international Working Group of the Society of Toxicologic Pathology (STP) recognizes the importance of addressing subjectivity and bias, and it has proposed methods to reduce their impact, in particular, discussing a necessary balance of adopting informed versus masked evaluation in animal toxicity studies. 6 Although mitigated by such principled protocols, when they are applied, subjectivity remains a concern and a major hurdle to the systematic and integrative analysis of the large amounts of animal study data that have been accumulated by industry and research organizations.

Notably, digital pathology workflows have enabled the systematic use of computer-assisted diagnosis tools to analyze digitized tissue specimens, called whole slide images (WSIs). Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies have already displayed the ability to solve several computational pathology tasks with high performance in clinical settings 7 -12 and could therefore improve animal pathology analysis by increasing data processing efficiency, accuracy, and the ability to identify lesions and characterize their environment with parametric quantification at the spatial level. However, the application of standard AI models such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in digital pathology requires large training and evaluation data sets and, most importantly, the availability of accurate pixel-level annotations to implement a direct lesion identification (e.g., lesion patterns of interest should be annotated at the pixel level within the WSI by pathologists, which is highly time-consuming). 2

The rapid advancement in image analysis with AI has been extensively evaluated in clinical applications, with very promising results. The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) recently cleared several AI-empowered software devices for the detection of potentially cancerous lesions. 13,14 However, clinical samples such as those resulting from surgical procedures are disease morphology dominant, and the difference between disease and control images is large. In toxicologic pathology evaluation for nonclinical safety studies, most tissues/organs lack test article-related change; 15 thus, animal pathology is normal morphology dominant and thus more challenging than clinical pathology. In addition, spontaneous injuries are possible in animal testing; it is critical to differentiate between spontaneous injuries and pathological injuries caused by testing chemicals. Stain heterogeneity caused by variations in slide preparation and image acquisition among different laboratories is also common. Further, WSIs are still too large to fit into GPU memory, and thus, WSIs must be practically partitioned into subimages (patches or tiles) of lower dimension, thus losing their spatial relationships. The need for preprocessing pipelines for color normalization, tissue region segmentation, and tile extraction must also be noted as a relevant source of variability and bias for AI models. A serious lack of reproducibility in preprocessing pipelines and the impact on AI models has been demonstrated in digital pathology tasks, with poor methodology leading to inflated results by data leakage. 16 For instance, in clinical pathology, an inflation of up to 41% of classification accuracy was estimated for designs that did not segregate tiles from the same patient either only in training or only in test. For toxicologic digital pathology, we introduce in this paper a careful data analysis design that keeps samples from the same treatment and topography together independently while training and evaluating the AI methods.

These concerns motivate the development of open-source frameworks for reproducible digital pathology preprocessing with automated testing such as the histolab Python library. 17

In this study, these issues were addressed with the priority of evolving AI systems that do not require pixel-level annotations. Although it is important to identify the location of specific lesions in the WSIs, pathology findings from WSIs are usually described through semistructured reports, without specifying a lesion's location. This level of pathology annotation is termed weak labels. In computational pathology applications, weakly

Table 1. Summary of the Data Sets Used in This Work

|Data set|Treated|Pathology finding|Severity score|Lesion spontaneity|Total WSIs|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|Control-NF|No|None|None|None|257|
|Control-F|No|Necrosis/Single cell necrosis|1 - 2|True|120|
|Mild-F SP|Yes|Necrosis/Single cell necrosis|1 - 2|True|157|
|Mild-F NSP|Yes|Necrosis/Single cell necrosis|1 - 2|False|167|
|Positive-F|Yes|Necrosis/Single cell necrosis|3 - 4|True ( n = 3) False ( n = 112)|115|


supervised approaches recover the annotation at slide level to train models on subimage collections, each tile obtaining the label of the original slide. Weakly supervised strategies can be successfully applied to training models for clinical tasks that do not require pixel-wise annotations such as tissue recognition 18 or tumor subtyping. 19 In animal pathology, however, normal histology on WSIs exceeds atypical patterns, which can be found at different locations and with different grades of severity 20 such that similar weakly supervised learning is highly likely to perform poorly in animal pathology. In addition, training classification models on tile sets require careful planning of the model training in order not to incur any unwanted biases such as data (or information) leakage. Whenever tiles extracted from the same WSI occur in both the training and the validation set, model results are heavily affected by overfitting. 16

Adapting the latest AI technologies to address these specific questions in animal pathology, we present PathologAI, an AI framework for the digital pathology analysis of WSIs from animal studies. Through training on WSIs given a pathology label assigned to the entire slide, PathologAI learns to recognize nonspontaneous injuries by their location and severity. The PathologAI framework is applied to predict necrosis on rat liver slides in an Open Toxicogenomics ProjectGenomics-Assisted Toxicity Evaluation system (TG-GATEs) on a total of 816 WSIs (377 controls). PathologAI used a weakly supervised classification approach and image embedding based on generative models. To boost reproducibility, PathologAI integrated the histolab 17 methods for reproducible WSI preprocessing, and stratified batch-effect free partitions were adopted in model training.

# ■ MATERIALS AND METHODS

The PathologAI Framework. The PathologAI architecture for weak label training was a generalization of the work of Tellez et al. 21 and was based on a combination of three main components (Figure 1):

Based on histolab functions, data sets of nonoverlapping square tiles were extracted from all the detected tissue on each WSI. An embedding map based on the deep learning Bidirectional Generative Adversarial Network (BiGAN) was trained to learn a low-dimensional representation of histological images at the tile level. Each tile was then compressed individually with the trained encoder, resulting in a data set of N vectors.

The embedding map was applied, resulting in a new image (actually a tensor) while preserving the original locality structure of the tissue. The positions corresponding to the background or empty tiles were filled with zero vectors. Each compressed WSI was further packed by removing empty rows and columns and adding padding of fixed size. The data set with the transformed images was stratified by label, balanced for topography type, and segregated by experiment, resulting in 5 splits that were used to train CNN classifiers (base estimators) with the WSI label as the target value.

Additionally, an ensemble of the 5 CNN base estimators was used as a final predictor. Ensemble models can mitigate the

impact of variations in the data distribution, with improved robustness and performance. Different uses of alternative classification heads and deep features analysis were also explored.

WSI Data Set. A total of 816 Liver WSIs from the TG-GATEs 22 public repository were utilized in this study. Digital images of the TGGATEs were reviewed by expert pathologists, and for each WSI, pathological annotations were retrieved including DOSE_LEVEL: the dose level of the test article (Control, Low, Middle, High); SINGLE_REPEAT_TYPE: the type of treatment exposure (single dose or repeat dose); FINDING_TYPE: the type of histological lesion; TOPOGRAPHY_TYPE: the region of the organ where the corresponding lesion was found; GRADE_TYPE: the severity of pathological changes based on a 4-point scoring system 17 (0: within normal limits, 1: minimal, 2: slight, 3: moderate, 4: severe); EXP_ID: unique identifier assigned to each test; and SP_FLG: the spontaneity of corresponding lesion (True/False). Note that EXP_ID was originally assigned as a combination 22 of COMPOUND_NAME (article), SPECIES (Sprague -Dawley rats in our data), EXP_TEST_-TYPE (in vivo or in vitro), and SINGLE_REPEAT_TYPE (singledose test or 28-day repeat dose test).

Necrosis or single cell necrosis (aggregated as necrosis in the rest of the paper) was selected as the target for drug induced liver injury (DILI). All images with liver necrosis available in the TG-GATEs database were used: we retrieved a total of 559 slides from treated or untreated rats with necrosis among pathological findings and 257 slides from control rats with no pathological findings identified. Of interest, 120 (21%) of the 559 WSIs with necrosis were found in untreated rats and labeled as spontaneous lesions (SP_FLG = True). The WSI collection was thus partitioned into five main subdata sets according to randomization group, lesion spontaneity, and lesion severity (Table 1):

Control-NF ( n = 257): WSIs of liver tissue from nontreated rats, no pathological changes annotated.

Control-F ( n = 120): WSIs of liver tissue from nontreated rats, necrosis was found among pathological findings with slight/ minimal GRADE_TYPE. Notice that all WSIs in this group had SP_FLG = True for the target lesion.

Mild-F SP ( n = 157): WSIs of liver tissue from treated rats, spontaneous necrosis (SP_FLG = True) was found among pathological findings with slight/minimal GRADE_TYPE.

Mild-F NSP ( n = 167): WSIs of liver tissue from treated rats, nonspontaneous necrosis (SP_FLG = False) was found among pathological findings with slight/minimal GRADE_TYPE.

Positive-F ( n = 115): WSIs of liver tissue from treated rats, test article related necrosis was found among pathological findings with moderate/severe GRADE_TYPE.

All the annotations from the histopathology examination are based on the original TG-GATEs guidance, where the authors used a consistent approach based on a 'Pathology Glossary', a consensuscontrolled vocabulary for histopathological findings for liver and kidney that was originally defined by NIHS. 22

Notice that when more than one target finding was detected on a single slide (e.g., for different topography types), we considered only the annotation with the highest severity score. Multiple annotated necrosis can occur on the same slide when Necrosis OR Single cell necrosis is repeatedly annotated on the same slide in the same or multiple regions (topography type). However, the impact of multiple annotated necrosis on the same slide is very small, occurring in <2%

of the total number of slides with Necrosis or Single cell necrosis findings (Figure S2).

WSIs were selected from the five data sets to address the main tasks of our experimental pipeline, namely the BiGAN training (102 WSIs), the necrosis classifier development (233 WSIs), and model testing (481 WSIs, Table 2 and Figure 2). For the Control-NF cases, we

Table 2. Data Sets Partitioned among BiGAN, CNN Model Training, and External Validation Data

|Data set|Number of WSIs|BiGAN|CNN training|CNN external validation|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|Control-NF|257|50|120|87|
|Control-F|120|0|0|120|
|Mild-F SP|157|10|0|147|
|Mild-F NSP|167|40|0|127|
|Positive-F|115|2|113|0|
|Total|816|102|233|481|

Figure 2. Diagram of data sets and tasks (see Table 2). Control-NF: control (DOSE = 0) with no pathology finding (NF). Control-F: control (DOSE = 0), with necrosis finding (F). Mild-F: treated (DOSE ≠ 0), with necrosis finding (F) and GRADE_TYPE slight or minimal. Positive-F: treated (DOSE ≠ 0), with target finding (F) and GRADE_TYPE moderate or severe. SP: spontaneous lesion. NSP: nonspontaneous lesion. For each data set, the number n of WSIs used for each task is indicated over the arrow connecting data set and corresponding task.

selected a subset of the slides in the TG-GATEs repository with no pathology findings ( n = 5856 total slides) to balance the number of necrotic slides per data set. Specifically:

25 control slides from the Single-dose test and 25 control slides from the 28-day repeat-dose test ( n = 50 total slides) were selected for the BiGAN encoder training.

120 control slides for CNN training were selected to match the distribution of sacrifice period of the positive cases.

87 control slides were randomly selected as external validation.

For reproducibility, the lists of slide names utilized for BiGAN and CNN development and for external validation are provided as Supporting Information files.

Training Data for the BiGAN Encoder. We selected a set of 102 WSIs including 50 WSIs from the Control-NF group and 52 WSIs from the treated group with the target finding of necrosis. In particular, the control group included 25 WSIs from the single dose time point and 25 WSIs from the repeat dose time point; the treated group consisted of 50 Mild-F slides evenly distributed on the topography type of the Positive-F data (Figure 3A), and 2 Positive-F slides including one WSI from the single dose group with (SP_FLG, DOSE_LEVEL) = (True, Middle), and the other from the repeat dose group with (SP_FLG, DOSE_LEVEL) = (False, High). From the WSI collection, we extracted a total of 1.08 M tiles of size 128 × 128 pixels used to train an embedding transformation based on a BiGAN model (see Materials and Methods).

Necrosis Classifier Data. The training data set for the necrosis classifier included 113 WSIs in the Positive-F group (the two WSIs used for the embedding network were excluded from the data set), described in terms of Finding, grade, and Topography type in Figure 3B. The training data set also includes 120 WSIs from the Control-NF group, for a total of 233 WSIs. The slides in the control group were selected to match (i) the 23 EXP_IDs of the positive group; (ii) the dose-time point (SACRIFICE_PERIOD) of the positive group, when available. Each slide was compressed with the trained BiGAN encoder, and the compressed slides were split into five training and validation sets corresponding to 80% and 20% of the total data set, respectively. To avoid data leakage effects, train test splits were stratified by label, balanced for topography type, and segregated by the original animal experiment (no EXP_ID is simultaneously in the training and test sets). Moreover, all of the validation sets have empty intersections.

The remaining 481 WSIs include the Control-F group ( n = 120), the Mild-F group excluded the slides used for the BiGAN training ( n = 274), and 87 WSIs from the Control-NF group with no EXP_ID in common with the training data. After compression and packing, these slides were used for model inference as the external validation data. The structure of the external validation data set is visually described in Figure S1. The impact of multiple annotated necrosis on the same slide for the external validation data set is analyzed in Figure S2.

Tile Extraction and Preprocessing. Tiling for the BiGAN Training. We extracted ∼ 10K nonoverlapping tiles from each WSI of the Control-NF ( n = 50) and the Mild-F groups ( n = 50) using the RandomTiler function of histolab, 17 and 85,000 tiles from the WSIs in the Positive group ( n = 2) using the GridTiler function of histolab. 17 Tiles were extracted at 20 × magnification (level 0) considering all of the tissue detected on the slide (TissueMask function), and only the tiles with at least 80% tissue were selected. The tissue detection method is described in the histolab paper reference (Marcolini et al. 17 ). The method is a sequence of image manipulation (grayscale conversion and Otsu thresholding) and morphological filters (binary dilation and small holes removal).

Tiling for CNN Training and External Validation. From each slide, N nonoverlapping tiles covering all the tissue detected were extracted at level 0 (min( N ) = 28,656, max( N ) = 85,104, mean( N ) = 52,299) using the GridTiler function with check_tissue = True to keep only the tiles with a minimum of 80% of tissue. All the extracted tiles were normalized with the Reinhard stain normalization method 23 using TCGA-A2-A3XS-DX11 as the target image. 24

Unsupervised Encoding Network. We adopted the BiGAN architecture 25 as the unsupervised encoding strategy to learn the mapping between the images and the embedding space. The BiGAN encoder had the best performance when used as a feature extractor for classifying necrotic tissue among the six evaluated encoders (three Variational Autoencoders, BiGAN, Siamese Network, mean-RGB embedding) compared in the work of Tellez et al. 21

The different encoders were used as a feature extractor for two supervised classification tasks, and the BiGAN architecture was superior on both tasks in terms of area under the curve (AUC) or accuracy with respect to a Variational Autoencoder, a contrastive training approach, and the standard embedding mean-RGB. In particular, on necrotic tissue classification, the BiGAN embeddings had the highest classification accuracy with a large improvement over other autoencoders.

In this study, we fixed the dimension of the latent space to C = 128. To avoid instability and mode collapse during training, we implemented several strategies including noise addition to both the real and synthetic data, one-sided label smoothing, 26 with labels of the real images sampled from a normal distribution with mean 0.8 and

Figure 3. Sunburst diagrams for FINDING_TYPE, SP_FLG, GRADE_TYPE, TOPOGRAPHY_TYPE, and EXP_ID for (A) the BiGAN training data with findings ( n = 52) and (B) the Positive-F group ( n = 115). For each cell, bold numbers indicate the total number of WSIs. Note: slides can include findings for both single cell Necrosis and Necrosis, typically at diverse topography.

![image 12](data:image/png;base64,)

(C) BiGAN synthetic tiles

Original tiles

(B) Original tiles after stain normalization

Figure 4. (A) Examples of tiles extracted from the BiGAN training data set before and (B) after the stain normalization and (C) examples of synthetic tiles generated by the trained BiGAN network.

variance 0.2, spectral normalization, 27 and two time-scale update rules. 28 Moreover, we exploited data augmentation techniques, i.e., random horizontal flipping and random rotation with degrees sampled from a uniform distribution with θ ∈ [ -π , -π /2, 0, π /2], and we finally resized the input image to half its size before feeding the model. The choice of the 128 × 128 fixed size for the BIGAN encoder follows previous empirical results, 21 which found this size an optimal balance between compression and histological detail scale. It is applied at the end of the preprocessing, including tissue detection and augmentation steps, thus providing a data preparation pipeline. The reduction to a coarse representation at the beginning of the process would indeed be different, for instance, averaging over more nontissue pixels or non-normalized tissue pixels. Notably, the process originates from a data tensor, where each pixel is a column of features, thus offering a much richer representation than a direct magnification reduction.

We minimized the binary cross entropy loss with the Adam optimizer with different learning rates (lr) for the discriminator (lr = 10 -4 ) and the generator (lr = 4 × 10 -4 ) and with fixed coefficients β 1 and β 2 = (0.5, 0.999). We trained the network with batch size 144 for a maximum of 2000 epochs and selected the encoder corresponding to the epoch with the lowest Fre chet Inception Distance (FID)

́

Table 3. Training and Internal Validation MCC for the 5 CNN Estimators of the Ensemble Model

|MCC|Model 1|Model 2|Model 3|Model 4|Model 5|Mean|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|Train|0.848|0.805|0.780|0.737|0.824|0.799|
|Internal validation|0.693|0.698|0.380|0.587|0.750|0.621|


# ■ RESULTS

value. 28 FID measures the quality of the generated images by computing the distance between the Inception-v3 activation distributions for real and generated samples. Examples of tiles generated with the BiGAN architecture are reported in Figure 4.

Classification Results. On the 5 internal validation sets (see Materials and Methods), classification results varied from MCC = 0.380 on Model 3 to MCC = 0.750 on Model 5 (Table 3). Notably, 176 WSIs were correctly classified on all models, including the six positive slides with a severe grade for necrosis, and n = 15 WSIs were misclassified in at least 4 models. Lower performance in validation was obtained when the target lesion was found in different regions (TOP-OGRAPHY_TYPE) than in the training slides. In particular, Model 3 is strongly unbalanced due to a large EXP_ID = 685 (corresponding to compound Methylenedianiline ); thus, slides with annotated lesions in bile duct cells were not learned during model training. As expected, the MCC and sensitivity of the training set were improved for the ensemble model (Table 4).

The weights of the encoder are learned independently from the injury score classification task, and the data set used for the BiGAN encoder has no intersection with the data used for training and validation of the classifiers, as specified in the following subsections, i.e., no further training or fine-tuning was applied to the encoder in backpropagation from the necrosis task to avoid data leakage effects.

Compression and Packing. As a variation to the Tellez work, 21 we included a 'packing and padding' step during WSI compression to cope with the general case of multiple, distinct slices of tissue on a single slide. First, each tile was independently compressed by the trained BiGAN model, resulting in a set of N ( N = number of extracted tiles) embedding vectors for each slide. To preserve the original spatial structure of the tissue in slide S, we filled a matrix of size HS/ h × WS/ w × c , where (HS, WS, 3) is the original size of S, ( h , w ) = (128, 128), and c = 128 is the dimension of the latent space, with the N vectors so that the embedding of a tile at position i , j on S occupies the ( i , j )th position of the matrix. The remaining positions of the matrix, corresponding to the background of the slide or to tiles that were not extracted, were filled with empty vectors of length c . As the slides in the TG-GATEs data set include two or more slices of tissue, often separated by empty background, we further removed the empty rows and columns of the matrix and added padding of fixed size 532, obtaining a data set of highly compact WSIs ( ∼ 1/10 of the original size). No tissue data but only background pixels were discarded in the compression process.

Table 4. Single and Ensemble Model Results for the Training Data Set ( n = 233) a

|Model|MCC|Sensitivity|Specificity|
|---|---|---|---|
|Single|0.764|0.887|0.877|
|Ensemble|0.794|0.912|0.883|


a Single model metrics are computed as the average of the metrics for the CNN estimators.

We quantitatively assessed the performance of the ensemble model on the external validation data by considering the positive class (necrosis) as the ground truth label for the MildF-NSP data and the negative class (Control) for the ControlNF, the Control-F, and the Mild-F-SP groups (Table 5).

CNN Architecture. For each of the five splits, the classifier was based on a CNN architecture composed of seven convolutional layers with 128 2-strided filters, batch normalization, leaky ReLu activation, 20% dropout, and stride 2, and a dense layer with sigmoid activation for the final classification. 21 We applied data augmentation strategies on both training and validation sets including random crop to size 512, random rotation where the angles were sampled from a uniform distribution with a set of discrete values θ ∈ [ -π , -π /2, 0, π /2], and random horizontal flip with p = 0.5. Moreover, we normalized the input channelwise with mean and standard deviation (std) computed on the training set. Specifically, let W = { W 0 , ..., W L } be the set of compressed WSIs in the training set, where Wi ∈ R H × W × C for i ∈ [1, ..., L ], and let Wic ∈ R H × W be the c th channel of Wi with mean value μ ic and std σ ic . We computed the mean and std per channel as μ c = mean( μ ic , ∀ i ∈ [1 ··· L ]}), and σ c = mean( σ ic , ∀ i ∈ [1 ··· L ]}). For each c = 1, ..., C , we then map xc ∈ Wic to = x c x c c c .

Table 5. Ensemble Model Accuracy on External Validation Data Sets

|Data set|# WSIs|Label|ACC (%)|
|---|---|---|---|
|Control-NF (test)|87|Control|87.4|
|Control-F|120|Control|82.5|
|Mild-F (SP)|147|Control|66.7|
|Mild-F (NSP)|127|Necrosis|58.7|


Moreover, the ensemble model classified Control-NF vs MildF-NSP slides with MCC = 0.48, and mild spontaneous (MildF-SP) vs mild treatment-related (Mild-F-NSP) injuries with MCC = 0.41.

We used Adam as the optimizer with an initial learning rate of 10 -2 and adopted the ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler, thus lowering the learning rate by a factor of 10 every time the training loss had not decreased for 30 epochs, up to the lower bound of 10 -8 . We maximized the binary cross-entropy loss and trained the classifier with batch size 24 for a maximum of 500 epochs, saving the checkpoint of the model with the highest Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) 29 value on the training set, after the learning curve converged and the MCC had only minimal oscillations due to the data augmentation on the training set.

To validate the approach, we applied a secondary analysis based on a Data Analysis Plan (DAP) providing an independent estimate of the final layers of the model, i.e., the classification head. The classification head was chosen by the DAP based on a 10 × 5 cross-validation resampling strategy. The DAP identifies the optimal model candidate as well as internal and external confidence intervals of the relevant classification metrics by means of bootstrap confidence interval of MCC) and a ranked list of features ranked by the Borda Method. Comparable results were found with the DAP, confirming the results with the CNN. The approach and results are reported in the Supporting Information.

Finally, we exploited the expected variability of the classifiers, which is due to the stratification procedure, to obtain a robust ensemble binary classification model from the average of the best models. We hypothesized that the ensemble model improves over the single CNN models on the internal and external validation data.

Spontaneous Lesion

Treatment-related Lesion

Figure 5. (A) Distribution of the Necrosis Score on classifier development and external validation data. (B) Violin plot of mean Necrosis Score on external validation data grouped by lesion spontaneity. SP: spontaneous; NSP: nonspontaneous.

~0.25

70.25

High

Control

Middle

Low

slight

moderate

minimal

Drug dose level

Lesion grade type

Figure 6. Violin plots of mean Necrosis Score (mNS) distribution on the external validation data grouped by (A) severity grade of target finding and (B) drug dose level. The Mann -Whitney-Wilcoxon test two-sided has been used for statistical comparison.

The PathologAI Ensemble Model Can Accurately Identify Treatment-Related Injury. We defined the Necrosis Score (NS) as the probability of the positive class output for each CNN classifier. The NS provides a quantitative evaluation of lesion severity, possibly more robust than a semiquantitative grading system that may affect the quality of toxicological assessments. 20 For clarity, we indicated with mNS the mean Necrosis Score derived as an average on the 5 CNN estimators trained on the 5 splits.

Although developed only on extreme end points, i.e., highseverity necrosis and control samples with no pathology findings, the PathologAI framework was effective in discerning between spontaneous and treatment-related injury (Figure

5B). Remarkably, the mNS applied on test slides in ControlNF, Control-F, and Mild-F-SP groups was distributed similarly to the Control-NF samples in the training set, while the mNS is higher for the Mild-F-NSP group (Figure 5A).

The PathologAI Ensemble Model Can Differentiate Severity of Injury and Drug Dosage. The mNS distribution on the external validation data demonstrated that the PathologAI framework was effective in predicting the severity level of the pathology finding, even with minimal and differentiating samples treated with diverse drug dosage (Figure 6). In particular, the mNS increased from minimal to moderate lesion severity (Figure 6A), and from control to a high level of drug dosage (Figure 6B).

Single

Repeat

Figure 7. Violin plots of mean Necrosis Score (mNS) distribution on the external validation TCGA data grouped by (A) dose-time points and (B) type of in vivo test (single or repeat). Single: 3 h, 6 h, 9 h, 24 h, Repeat: 4 day, 8 day, 15 day, 29 day. The Mann -Whitney-Wilcoxon test two-sided has been used for statistical comparison.

The PathologAI Ensemble Model Can Differentiate Type of Dose-Time Points. The mNS distribution on the external validation data was also used to investigate possible impact of the characteristic dose-time points (Figure 7). For repeat dosage, the mNS scores increased from 3 to 9 h (Figure 7A). Notably, the PathologAI ensemble model significantly distinguished between single and repeat dose types (Figure 7B).

# ■ DISCUSSION

The histopathology analysis of tissue samples is the benchmark for the chemical safety assessment in toxicology. Various AI models have been successfully applied to address relevant tasks in toxicology, primarily using tiles extracted from WSIs to train deep learning architectures. These models often adopt a 'strong' label approach, relying on the pixel-level annotation of lesions. For instance, Pischon et al. 30 exploited a U-Net architecture to segment hepatocellular hypertrophy on liver samples from rats treated with phenobarbital. A total of 34 hematoxylin and eosin stained WSIs from 8 different studies were used, and manually selected regions of interest (ROIs) were annotated by pathologists. Mudry et al. 31 trained a Visual Geometry Group (VGG) network to quantify rodent retinal toxicity by determining nuclear layer thickness, where manually annotated tiles of different severity grades were extracted from 112 WSIs for model training. Tokarz et al. 32 developed a deep learning algorithm to segment and score the severity of progressive cardiomyopathy (PCM)-related abnormalities, classifying necrosis, fibrosis, mononuclear cell infiltration, and mineralization based on tile annotation. However, this pixellevel or tile-driven annotation requires input from board certified experts, which can be expensive and labor-intensive. Animal pathology often involves the WSIs being annotated with classification labels at the whole slide level, which leads to a weakly supervised classification scenario. Therefore, we specifically designed PathologAI as an AI-based framework for chemical-induced necrosis classification in a weak-label setting. The framework also includes a careful stratification design of model training to mitigate data leakage effects.

A collection of weakly supervised deep learning pipelines for WSI classification in computational pathology was benchmarked by Laleh et al. 33 Vision transformers were suggested as a potential new class of highly performing models. Beyond digital pathology, weakly supervised deep learning has also been used in precision oncology on somatic single nucleotide variants (SNV). 34 Weakly supervised workflows outperformed multiple-instance learning (MIL)-based weakly supervised methods for mutation prediction. Most of these applications are in clinical settings where the diseased tissues are markedly different from the normal tissues in morphology. However, healthy tissue in histopathology slides for animal studies usually exceeds diseased tissue, or multiple lesion types of progressively increasing severity may be present in the same slide. The former point was also noted in previous work based on supervised AI techniques for toxicologic evaluation on rat pathology WSIs. 35 PathologAI was developed to classify samples from extreme end points (treated samples with moderate/severe injury vs controls with no injuries); remarkably, the trained model was able to differentiate spontaneous (SP) injury from the test article-related injury. This could be useful in real applicative contexts, where the presence of SP lesions could lead to false positives; furthermore, these results are suggestive that the presence of SP lesions in the training data might introduce an intermediate subgroup with an impact on the resulting classifiers. We suggest that this observation is not well discussed in the literature and could contribute to developing better models in similar contexts. Moreover, PathologAI can stratify samples according to lesion severity level, even between mild findings and dose-dependent experimental design. The model appears to capture the amount of toxicologic burden in terms of drug dosage or type of dose-time points, thus agreeing with the common sense that increasing drug-dose levels increase the burden. As the relation between the drug-dose level and the lesion grade can be complex, it is of interest that the model can retrospectively confirm or reconstruct an increase of exposure. This fact is of potential interest in unknown exposure studies.

The availability of PathologAI contributes to addressing the overarching question of whether AI can perform as humans or

better when it comes to animal digital pathology. For example, the availability of an automated tool for quantitative lesion detection opens the possibility of scaling up the reuse of large preclinical pathology data collections. Moreover, the AI features can enable some uniform and integrative analysis of the existing animal data across different readouts, e.g., toxicogenomic mRNA profiles. Such analysis will provide a rich knowledge base and open the door to developing alternative assays with less impact on animals or which are wholly nonanimal based. As a caution, we observe that this study is limited to the use of data extracted from the TGGATEs data set. This is a limitation but also an advantage because this ensures a consistent use of protocols and pathology review procedures. Moreover, we have been able to design a stratification that is not so usually considered and that can be exploited in the pairing with integrated genomics data set. However, the availability of a larger data set for training would enable an ablation study informing on the relevance of training data sizes. 36

There are several directions for future work. Chen et al. designed the ToxGAN approach to generate transcriptomic profiles for toxicogenomics studies without animal experiments. 37 We plan to integrate ToxGAN with PathologAI in a GAN-based framework for the spatial location of gene expression signatures on histopathology images. Additionally, PathologAI will be trained on the more general task of discriminating normal vs abnormal histology defined by aggregation of multiple lesion types in a single class as a step toward pinpointing more accurate toxicological profiles. A comparison with effective MIL methods 36 on a large-scale experiment in the context of generalized lesion classification would also be of strong interest. In particular, how different frameworks can deal with patterns present at different scales and the variation of the spatial distribution among patches (global patterns) is a question to be investigated. As a further current limit and focus of further work, this paper has not discussed the use of PathologAI for explanatory purposes and direct support of pathology work. Notably, we considered single cell necrosis, which is less immediate to pinpoint than larger necrotic areas and thus would require an annotated resource not available in TG-GATEs. We plan to expand this feature in further work, possibly with the use of integrated omics information.

Despite the adoption of fair evaluation frameworks, more sophisticated methods are needed to assess the reproducibility of AI pipelines on biomedical data. In particular, data and concept drifts must be evaluated before deployment, considering the adoption of approaches such as online training, or focused retraining of models.
